_comment: Hand-maintained editorial layer. No live feed exists for these per the tech spec (WGC gold demand, cross-bloc penalty, welfare scenarios, bloc cohesion/notes, dispatch). Merged last by the harvester so these values always win over anything scraped. Seeded from the existing dashboard's 2025-26 era -- update quarterly.

cb_gold_tonnes_4q.source: World Gold Council Gold Demand Trends (hand-entered)

west.note: A wave of tariffs and investment restrictions — the deepest fragmentation in decades. Each wall creates winners who defend it.

east.note: Official gold holdings pass foreign-held Treasuries for the first time in generations. The single anchor drops below half.

nonaligned.note: Two-thirds of world trade now runs through value chains being actively reshaped. The middle is now the system's remaining shock absorber.

dispatch: The storm arrives not as one blow but as a thousand small closures, each with its own constituency sworn to keep it shut. Gold in the vaults now outweighs the paper promises. Below half — for the first time, the city asks what holds the generator up.

scenarios.desc: The blocs settle into a cold but stable two-system world. Incomplete fragmentation costs -1.9% of world welfare — the intra/inter-bloc gap holds at its step-change rather than spiralling. The middle stays unclaimed, and keeps the two halves connected.

Restrictions create concentrated domestic winners, and winners defend walls. Each closure is politically impossible to unwind, so the ratchet only turns one way. The 2025-26 tariff wave reopens the gap and the system drifts toward the worst projections.
The middle picks the incumbent order. Modelled world welfare loss deepens to -2.7% — the shock absorber becomes a wall, and the East is boxed into a smaller, harder bloc with less to lose.
The middle tilts to the challenger. The costliest path modelled: -3.7% of world welfare. The incumbent financial system loses its periphery, and the two halves stop sharing anything worth protecting.
